standard_description: Measure and estimate liquid volumes and masses of objects using standard units of grams (g), kilograms (kg), and liters (l). Add, subtract, multiply, or divide to solve one-step word problems involving masses or volumes that are given in the same units.
sources: NCTM Progressions for the Common Core State Standards in Mathematics — Geometric Measurement K–5, 2012
Van de Walle, J.A., Karp, K.S., & Bay-Williams, J.M. (2013). Elementary and Middle School Mathematics (8th ed.). Pearson.
EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Topics D-E — Mass and Liquid Volume, Teacher Edition, 2013
Lehrer, R. (2003). Developing understanding of measurement. In Kilpatrick, J. et al. (Eds.), A Research Companion to Principles and Standards for School Mathematics. NCTM.
Smarter Balanced Assessment Consortium — Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications, 2015

misconceptions.short_label: Wrong unit choice (gram vs kilogram, liter vs ml)
misconceptions.description: Student chooses the wrong unit when estimating mass or volume: uses grams for heavy objects (e.g., a backpack = 5 g) or kilograms for tiny objects (e.g., a paper clip = 1 kg). Similarly confuses ml and L for liquid volume.
misconceptions.example: Estimate the mass of a textbook. Student answers 2 g (should be ~2 kg). Or: estimate water in a bathtub. Student answers 100 ml (should be ~100 L).
misconceptions.citation: Lehrer (2003) p.184 — 'Children\'s ability to choose appropriate measurement units develops slowly; without explicit benchmark anchors (a paper clip = 1 g, a textbook ≈ 1 kg, a water bottle ≈ 1 L), they default to the unit most recently taught.'
misconceptions.remediation: Build benchmark anchors: paper clip ≈ 1 g, dollar bill ≈ 1 g, textbook ≈ 1 kg, dog ≈ 10-20 kg. For volume: teaspoon ≈ 5 ml, water bottle ≈ 500 ml, soda bottle ≈ 2 L. Compare unknown object to nearest benchmark.

misconceptions.short_label: Wrong operation in mass/volume word problem
misconceptions.description: Student picks the wrong operation (addition vs subtraction, multiplication vs division) in a mass or volume word problem. Common when keyword translation fails (e.g., 'altogether' triggers addition even when subtraction is needed).
misconceptions.example: 'A jug holds 8 L. After pouring out some water, 3 L remain. How much was poured?' Student adds 8 + 3 = 11 L instead of subtracting 8 − 3 = 5 L.
misconceptions.citation: EngageNY G3 Module 2 Lesson 10 Teacher Edition — 'Word problems involving mass and volume present the same operation-selection challenges as other word problem types. Drawing a bar model or labeling "start, change, end" prevents keyword-based misinterpretation.'
misconceptions.remediation: Draw a bar model. Identify what is known and what is unknown. 'Start' → 'End' with arrow shows change direction (add or subtract). For 'each of n containers holds k liters', use multiplication; for 'split equally', use division.

misconceptions.short_label: Misreading intermediate marks on a scale
misconceptions.description: Reading a balance or measuring cup, student misjudges values that fall between marked tick increments. Often reports the value of the nearest mark instead of estimating to the nearest unit.
misconceptions.example: Measuring cup shows 200 ml and 300 ml tick marks; water level is just above 200 ml. Student reads 300 ml (next mark) instead of 220 ml.
misconceptions.citation: Van de Walle et al. (2013) p.412 — 'Students struggle to interpolate between marked tick values on measuring instruments, defaulting to the nearest large mark; teaching them to identify how many sub-divisions exist between major marks resolves this.'
misconceptions.source: Van de Walle Elementary and Middle School Mathematics
misconceptions.remediation: Count sub-divisions: 'between 200 and 300, I see 10 small marks → each = 10 ml.' Practice reading scales with various tick spacings.

misconceptions.description: Student conflates mass (g, kg) with volume (ml, L). Believes a heavier object always takes more space, or that two objects with the same volume have the same mass.
misconceptions.example: 'A balloon and a bowling ball have the same mass.' Student says yes if both 'look similar size.' Or: estimates mass of water by its volume in ml directly (1 L ≠ 1 kg always, depends on liquid).
misconceptions.citation: Lehrer (2003) p.187 — 'Distinguishing mass (amount of matter) from volume (space occupied) is conceptually difficult; children often equate "big" with "heavy" and vice versa, ignoring density.'
misconceptions.remediation: Demonstrate with examples: a pillow (large volume, small mass) vs a brick (small volume, large mass). Mass = weight on a scale. Volume = space inside a container.

misconceptions.short_label: Omitting or using wrong units in the answer
misconceptions.description: Student computes correctly but reports the number without units, or attaches the wrong unit (e.g., grams to a volume answer).
misconceptions.example: Mass problem answer 'The total is 350.' (no unit). Or '350 ml' for a mass answer.
misconceptions.citation: NCTM GM Progressions K-5 (2012) p.14 — 'Reporting answers without units (or with wrong units) is a persistent error in measurement problems; explicit attention to "What unit does the answer need?" before computing reduces this.'
misconceptions.remediation: Before computing, write the unit (g, kg, ml, L) at the end of the answer line. After computing, re-read the question to confirm unit matches.

misconceptions.short_label: Unrealistic estimates (off by orders of magnitude)
misconceptions.description: Student estimates mass or volume by an order of magnitude wrong, indicating no benchmark sense. Example: 1 ml for a swimming pool, or 1000 kg for a pencil.
misconceptions.example: Estimate water in a swimming pool. Student answers 100 ml (should be hundreds of thousands of liters).
misconceptions.citation: Van de Walle et al. (2013) p.412 — 'Without benchmark experiences, students produce estimates that are wildly inconsistent with reality; building "reference quantities" through repeated hands-on activities is essential before formal measurement work.'
misconceptions.source: Van de Walle Elementary and Middle School Mathematics
misconceptions.remediation: Sense check: compare to a familiar benchmark. Pool >> bathtub (~150 L) >> bucket (~10 L) >> water bottle (~1 L). If estimate violates the ordering, reconsider.

misconceptions.short_label: Mixing units in a single calculation
misconceptions.description: Student adds or subtracts quantities with different units (e.g., grams + kilograms) without converting first.
misconceptions.example: 'A box has 2 kg of apples and 500 g of pears. Total mass?' Student answers 502 (mixing kg and g) instead of converting first: 2 kg = 2000 g + 500 g = 2500 g (or 2.5 kg).
misconceptions.citation: NCTM GM Progressions K-5 (2012) p.15 — 'Mixed-unit addition is a common error; students must convert all quantities to a single unit before computing. Explicit unit-conversion practice precedes mixed-unit problems.'
misconceptions.remediation: Step 1: write both quantities in the SAME unit (usually convert larger → smaller, or vice versa). Step 2: add/subtract. Step 3: optionally convert back to a sensible unit.
